A personalised map of what your body and mind have been carrying.
Prepared forSarah
Date15 July 2026
Report typeScreening Report

There is no rush. Take your time with this.
You are safe to go at your own pace.

This is your map.

You have just answered questions about parts of your life that are often difficult to describe.

This is not a diagnosis. It is not a judgement. It is not a measure of how strong, capable, or resilient you are.

Think of it as a map. A way of seeing patterns that may have been shaping how you feel, how you think, how you move through your days, and how safe life feels inside your body.

Some parts may feel accurate. Some may feel less relevant to you. Screening tools show patterns, not the whole person. Take your time with what resonates.

This report cannot replace a conversation with a qualified professional. It is a starting point, not a final word.

How this map is structured

This map moves through five areas of your experience:

  • The Loadhow much your system has been carrying recently
  • The Foghow life has been feeling from the inside
  • The Body Roomyour relationship with your body's signals
  • The Weight You Carrywhat past experiences may still be echoing
  • The Weather Insideyour emotional patterns and protection responses

Within each section you will find a personalised reflection drawn from your specific answers, a view of what your nervous system may be trying to do, and a research-grounded interpretation drawn from the published science behind each tool.

The five tools used in this screening are the PSS-10, PHQ-8, MAIA-2, PCL-5, and PID-5-SF, validated instruments used in clinical research and practice worldwide. Your results are measured against published population data.

Your full responses are included in the addendum at the end of this map.

If anything here feels difficult, pause. You do not need to read this all at once.
Section 1 of 5
The Load
PSS-10
This part of the map looks at how much your system has been carrying recently. Not what happened. Simply how much pressure your body and mind have been trying to hold.

Higher than 88% of adults in the general population

High stress load

(Cohen & Janicki-Deverts, 2012)

Your answers suggest life currently feels heavier than it should. There is a sense of managing one thing while already worrying about the next. Alongside this, a growing uncertainty about your own capacity, not because you are incapable, but because carrying too much for too long changes how capable you feel.

What we noticed

Problems do not arrive one at a time right now. They arrive in clusters. By the time one is managed, another has already appeared. The effort to stay on top of things appears constant, even when it is not visible to anyone else.

What your nervous system may be trying to do

When demands remain high for long enough, the system reorganises around one goal: get through today. From the inside this often feels like survival rather than participation. Many people interpret this as failure. It is not. It is a system that has been working extremely hard, often without enough rest between demands.

Here is what the research says about this pattern.

Research-grounded interpretation

In this range, stress appears to have become something closer to a constant presence. Life may feel unpredictable and difficult to control, and the sense that difficulties are piling up faster than they can be managed is familiar. This is not a character flaw. It is a physiological and psychological response to sustained pressure that has exceeded the system's current capacity to absorb it.

High perceived stress has been linked in research with changes in stress physiology, immune function, and recovery processes (Cohen & Janicki-Deverts, 2012). The body and the mind do not separate stress from its physical toll. What you are carrying is real, significant, and worth taking seriously.

Your answers also suggest that the stress leans toward doubting your own capacity to navigate what life is bringing. When this pattern is present, even manageable demands can feel threatening. Rebuilding that sense of capacity through small, concrete experiences of managing and succeeding is often the most direct route through (Bandura, 1997).

Section 2 of 5
The Fog
PHQ-8
This part of the map looks at how life has been feeling from the inside over the past two weeks, including mood, energy, sleep, and how you have been experiencing yourself.
97th

Higher than 97% of adults

Moderately severe
None / minimal
76%
Mild
18%
Moderate
5%
Your result
97th

(Kroenke et al., 2009)

Your answers suggest more than sadness. They suggest depletion. A tiredness that rest does not fully solve. A heaviness that changes how the world feels from the inside. Activities that once felt natural now require a decision. Simple things carry more weight than they should.

What we noticed

Getting through the day appears to take more effort than it used to. The gap between knowing you should do something and actually being able to do it has grown. This is not about motivation. It is about capacity.

What your nervous system may be trying to do

When the body has spent a long time under pressure, it often begins conserving energy. From the inside this can feel like: "I know I should care more than I do." This is not laziness. It is a system attempting to protect itself from further overload. Exhausted systems can recover. They need the right conditions to do so.

Here is what the research says about this pattern.

Research-grounded interpretation

Symptoms in this range are not occasional or mild. They appear consistent, pervasive, and affecting the ability to function across most areas. Getting through the day may require significant effort, and things that used to feel manageable may now feel harder.

Scores in this range are commonly associated with clinically significant depressive symptoms and substantial disruption to daily functioning. This does not diagnose depression, but it does suggest that professional support would be appropriate (Kroenke et al., 2009). Research consistently shows that symptoms at this level respond well to treatment. It is not a fixed state. It is a point in a trajectory that can change with the right support.

The physical dimensions of your answers are particularly prominent: sleep, energy, appetite. Depressive symptoms live in the body as much as in thoughts and feelings. Approaching them through the body's experience tends to be effective for this pattern (Kirmayer, 2001).

Section 3 of 5
The Body Room
MAIA-2
This part of the map looks at your relationship with your body's signals, how you notice them, whether you can stay with them, and whether your body feels like a source of information or something to move away from.
Not avoiding body sensations
18th
Tolerating strong feelings
24th
Seeing yourself clearly
31st
Emotional awareness
42nd
Self-regulation
14th

(Mehling et al., 2018)

One of the strongest themes in your results appears to be distance from your body. Not disconnection exactly. More like the body's signals arrive muffled, or arrive and are quickly redirected. Discomfort is managed by moving attention elsewhere. This is very common. And it usually developed for a reason.

What we noticed

There appears to be a quality of distance from your own body. Discomfort is managed by moving attention away from it rather than toward it. This is a common survival strategy, especially when emotions or sensations have felt overwhelming in the past.

What your nervous system may be trying to do

When the body has been associated with pain, threat, or overwhelm, the system learns to spend as little time there as possible. Staying in the head, keeping sensation at arm's length: these are protection strategies, not personal failings. The capacity to reconnect is still there. It has simply gone quiet.

Here is what the research says about this pattern.

Not avoiding body sensations

When the body signals discomfort, the default response may be to redirect attention elsewhere, to push through, to occupy the mind with something else. This is a common pattern, especially when body experience has historically been associated with pain or overwhelm. It is not a character trait. It is a learned protective response that can, with care, be changed (Mehling et al., 2018).

Self-regulation

The body may not currently be available as a tool for managing distress. When things become overwhelming, turning attention to the body or breath may not produce a sense of calm. This does not mean regulation is impossible. It means this particular route may currently be less accessible.

Low self-regulation stands out because it may affect how easily body-based strategies can be used during support. If turning toward the body increases distress, the first step is usually not deeper body awareness, but more safety. This is a sequencing consideration, not a ceiling (Mehling et al., 2018; Van der Kolk, 2014).

Section 4 of 5
The Weight
You Carry
PCL-5
What you named before this section
"The end of my marriage, three years ago."
This part of the map looks at whether difficult or distressing experiences from the past are still taking up space inside your system today.
84th

Your responses show more impact from difficult experiences than 84% of adults

Moderate
0th25th50th75th100th
MinimalModerate to Severe

(Renyer, 2016; Bovin et al., 2016)

Your answers suggest this experience may not simply be a memory. Parts of it appear to still be active within your system. Not necessarily because you think about it constantly, but because the body remembers experiences differently than the mind. Certain emotions, certain situations, certain reminders may still activate responses connected to what happened.

What we noticed

The experience you named appears to still feel close in your system. Not necessarily as a constant thought, but as a presence. Certain situations, emotions, or moments activate something that feels connected to it.

What your nervous system may be trying to do

One way to understand this is that your system may still be responding as if parts of what happened are not fully settled. Still watching. Still bracing in certain situations. From the inside this can feel like: "I know I am overreacting. I cannot seem to stop." This is not irrational. It is a system protecting you from something it learned was dangerous. It has simply not yet received enough evidence that the danger has passed.

Here is what the research says about this pattern.

Research-grounded interpretation

Symptoms appear to be present at a meaningful level. Re-experiencing, avoidance, mood changes, and heightened alertness may be affecting daily life with some consistency. This level of symptom burden warrants attention (Renyer, 2016).

The most prominent symptoms in your answers appear to involve changes in how you feel and think: persistent low mood, a changed sense of yourself, difficulty experiencing positive feelings, and a sense of distance from others. These are not fixed conclusions. They are among the most directly addressable aspects of this pattern with the right support (Ehlers & Clark, 2000).

Section 5 of 5
The Weather
Inside
PID-5-SF
This part of the map looks at your emotional patterns, how you tend to respond under pressure, how you relate to others, and the ways your system has learned to protect itself.
96th
22nd
68th
Negative emotional
reactivity
Withdrawing from
relationships
Impulse and
self-control
Negative emotional reactivity
Strongly elevated
Withdrawing from relationships
Typical range
Impulse and self-control
Elevated

(Miller et al., 2022)

Your emotional world appears intense. Feelings seem to arrive quickly and stay longer. They may require more energy to manage than most people experience. This does not mean you are too sensitive. It means your emotional system may currently be operating at a higher volume than most.

What we noticed

Emotions appear to arrive fast and feel more intense than you would like. The gap between something happening and your emotional response may be shorter than for most people. This is tiring, and it often feels like a reaction you would rather not be having.

What your nervous system may be trying to do

The system may have a lower threshold for emotional activation than most. From the inside this can feel like: "I know this is not a big deal. Why am I reacting like this?" The system is not being dramatic. It has likely learned to feel things quickly and strongly, often as a form of protection. Sensitivity and reactivity are not weaknesses. They are learned responses. They can change.

Here is what the research says about this pattern.

Negative emotional reactivity

Intense, frequent, and diverse negative emotions appear to be a central feature of daily life. Emotional experiences may feel more powerful and harder to manage than for most people. This pattern stands out as one of the strongest signals in your results.

Scores above this range are strongly elevated compared with published norms and suggest that emotional intensity is one of the clearest patterns in this map (Miller et al., 2022). This level is commonly associated with difficulty regulating emotional responses and heightened sensitivity to relational signals.

Impulse and self-control

There may be a tendency to act quickly on urges without always pausing to consider outcomes, and a difficulty maintaining sustained focus. This pattern is often connected to emotional intensity: when feelings move fast, actions tend to follow (Miller et al., 2022).

What the map seems to show

Across all five sections, one pattern appears again and again.

Your system appears to be carrying more than it currently has capacity to comfortably hold. The stress. The exhaustion. The distance from your body. The experience that still appears to be echoing. The emotional intensity that seems to take significant daily effort to manage. These are not five separate problems. They appear to be different expressions of the same underlying story: a nervous system that has spent a long time adapting, protecting, and trying to keep going under sustained pressure.

What stands out most is the combination of high emotional intensity and limited body-based regulation. When emotions are loud and the body is not available as a settling tool, there may be nowhere for the activation to go. This may be one reason why the load feels so relentless, even in moments when nothing specific is happening.

The experience you named in Section 4 does not appear to sit apart from the rest of the map. It appears to be woven through it, influencing mood, energy, how safe the body feels, and how close people can get.

A questionnaire can show patterns. It cannot show which experiences mattered most, or which ones are still the loudest, or what your nervous system most needs in order to begin to settle.

That is not a limitation of this map. It is simply the difference between a map and a conversation.

You have done something real by completing this. You have looked clearly at something most people avoid. The Clarity Call is the natural next step, not because something is wrong, but because you now have information worth working with. Bring your questions, bring what surprised you, bring whatever feels heaviest. That is exactly what the call is for.

The map you are holding tells you what is present. The call tells you what to do with it. That is the difference between understanding your situation and beginning to change it.

This map was generated by The Bridge Hub screening tool on 15 July 2026.

The Bridge Hub uses five validated psychological instruments, the PSS-10, PHQ-8, MAIA-2, PCL-5, and PID-5-SF, scored against published population norms. Results are for informational purposes only and do not constitute a clinical diagnosis. This report cannot replace a conversation with a qualified professional.

Your responses are stored securely in accordance with our privacy policy.
